what: The problem: unequal precision across the calibration curve
title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: Immunoassay measurements determined via calibration curve. observation posterior coefficient variation (pcov) quantifies precisely curve determines concentration. Observations near limits quantification large pcov values; midrange observations small pcov. Traditional approaches handle binary gate: include LLOQ ULOQ, exclude otherwise. discards information treats included observations equally precise. curveRweights replaces continuous, data-estimated precision weights via Bayesian location-scale model.

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: example_assay dataset contains 48,224 observations Luminex multiplex immunoassay: 11 antigens, 10 features, 150 subjects, 4 timepoints, across 15 plates.
code: library(curveRweights) library(dplyr) #> Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.5.2 #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union data(example_assay) str(example_assay) #> 'data.frame':    48224 obs. of  11 variables: #>  $ plate                  : chr  "plate_1" "plate_1" "plate_1" "plate_1" ... #>  $ nominal_sample_dilution: chr  "dil_6" "dil_6" "dil_6" "dil_6" ... #>  $ patientid              : chr  "S013" "S013" "S013" "S013" ... #>  $ group_a                : chr  "vaccine_b" "vaccine_b" "vaccine_b" "vaccine_b" ... #>  $ group_b                : chr  "timepoint_3" "timepoint_3" "timepoint_3" "timepoint_3" ... #>  $ antigen                : chr  "act" "dt" "ipv1" "ipv2" ... #>  $ feature                : chr  "IgG1" "IgG1" "IgG1" "IgG1" ... #>  $ mfi                    : num  43 1034 101 85 1169 ... #>  $ predicted_concentration: num  1.2 39.58 8.74 1.33 11.9 ... #>  $ se_concentration       : num  0.1546 3.5301 0.5288 0.0633 1.1052 ... #>  $ pcov                   : num  0.1767 0.0789 0.102 0.0405 0.1409 ...

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: prepare_cv() computes outcome variable (yi = log10(concentration)) precision index (cv_i), preferring stored pcov calibration curve simpler se / concentration ratio.
code: # Focus on IgG1 responses to pertussis antigens dat_igg1 <- example_assay |>   filter(feature == "IgG1", antigen %in% c("pt", "fha", "prn"))  dat_igg1 <- prepare_cv(dat_igg1, pcov_col = "pcov") head(dat_igg1[, c("antigen", "patientid", "yi", "cv_i", "log_cv", "cv_source")]) #>   antigen patientid         yi       cv_i    log_cv cv_source #> 1     prn      S013  0.9707595 0.11265055 -2.183465      pcov #> 2      pt      S013 -0.1981600 0.04432566 -3.116192      pcov #> 3     fha      S013 -0.1086106 0.07826967 -2.547595      pcov #> 4     prn      S013  0.4622342 0.12126340 -2.109790      pcov #> 5      pt      S013 -0.3222110 0.04972253 -3.001297      pcov #> 6     fha      S013 -0.3375811 0.08815158 -2.428697      pcov

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: fitting, check whether pcov enough variation identify precision exponent (beta1). sd(log_cv) < 0.05, scale model falls back uniform weights.
code:
# Check one antigen d_prn <- dat_igg1 |> filter(antigen == "prn") cv_diag <- diagnose_cv(d_prn) cat(cv_diag$message, "
") #> OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.487; beta1 identifiable from 506 observations cat("cv range:", cv_diag$cv_min, "to", cv_diag$cv_max, "
") #> cv range: 0.1056 to 3.12 cat("sd(log_cv):", cv_diag$log_cv_sd, "
") #> sd(log_cv): 0.4872

what: Step 3: Create the saturated cell-means factor
title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: key design principle cell factor created externally. gives full control constitutes “cell” experiment. dataset, cells defined combination vaccine group (group_a) timepoint (group_b): cell gets mean location model (yi ~ 0 + cell). scale model (log(sigma) ~ log_cv) shared across cells, estimated ~1,500 observations instead ~150.
code: dat_igg1$cell <- interaction(dat_igg1$group_a, dat_igg1$group_b, drop = TRUE) table(dat_igg1$cell) #>  #> vaccine_a.timepoint_1 vaccine_b.timepoint_1 vaccine_a.timepoint_2  #>                   195                   216                   123  #> vaccine_b.timepoint_2 vaccine_a.timepoint_3 vaccine_b.timepoint_3  #>                   126                   222                   228  #> vaccine_a.timepoint_4 vaccine_b.timepoint_4  #>                   201                   225

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: fit_saturated_weight_batch() fits one model per group (, per antigen). group gets phi beta1 different antigens may different pcov--variance relationships. typical scale_table three antigens might look like: Key observations: fha phi near 1 beta1 < 1: calibration curve well calibrated pcov slightly overstates precision gradient. prn largest phi (excess variance) beta1 near 2: pcov strongly drives weight differentiation, observations high pcov heavily downweighted. pt close theoretical prediction (phi ~ 1, beta1 ~ 1).
code: batch <- fit_saturated_weight_batch(   datg       = dat_igg1,   group_vars = "antigen",   cell_col   = "cell",   pcov_col   = "pcov",   plate_col  = "plate",   iter       = 4000,   warmup     = 1000,   chains     = 4,   cores      = 4 ) batch$scale_table

what: Step 5: Extract weights for a specific comparison
title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: fitting, weights attached every row data. Filter subset downstream analysis:
code: # Compare vaccine_b vs vaccine_a at timepoint_3 dat_comparison <- batch$data |>   filter(group_a %in% c("vaccine_a", "vaccine_b"),          group_b == "timepoint_3")  # Weights are ready summary(dat_comparison$w_saturated_norm)

what: Step 6: Downstream arm-effect testing with metafor
title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: weights can used directly metafor::rma(). phi already captures residual heterogeneity (tau-squared analog), use method = "FE" avoid double-counting: key output group_avaccine_b coefficient: estimated difference z-scored concentration vaccine_b vaccine_a, weighted calibration-curve precision.
code: library(metafor)  # For one antigen at timepoint_3 dat_prn_t3 <- batch$data |>   filter(antigen == "prn", group_b == "timepoint_3",          group_a %in% c("vaccine_a", "vaccine_b"))  # z-score the outcome within this comparison dat_prn_t3$conc_z <- scale(dat_prn_t3$predicted_concentration)  # vi = sigma_i^2 = 1 / w_saturated dat_prn_t3$vi <- 1 / dat_prn_t3$w_saturated  # Set reference level dat_prn_t3$group_a <- factor(dat_prn_t3$group_a,                               levels = c("vaccine_a", "vaccine_b"))  # Fixed-effects meta-regression (tau2 = 0 by design) fit_rma <- rma(   yi   = conc_z,   vi   = vi,   mods = ~ group_a,   data = dat_prn_t3,   method = "FE" )  summary(fit_rma)

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: nonparametric arm comparison respects precision weights, use survey::svyranktest():
code: library(survey)  dat_prn_t3$conc_z <- scale(dat_prn_t3$predicted_concentration)  des <- svydesign(ids = ~1, weights = ~w_saturated, data = dat_prn_t3) rt  <- svyranktest(conc_z ~ group_a, design = des) rt

what: Step 8: Apply saved weights to new data
title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: receive new data assay platform, apply previously estimated scale parameters without re-fitting:
code: # Save the scale_table from the batch fit saveRDS(batch$scale_table, "scale_table_IgG1.rds")  # Later, with new data: scale_table <- readRDS("scale_table_IgG1.rds")  dat_new_weighted <- apply_saturated_weights(   df          = dat_new,   scale_table = scale_table,   group_vars  = "antigen",   pcov_col    = "pcov" )

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: two scale parameters direct scientific meaning: phi = exp(gamma_0) baseline scaling factor. phi = 1: pcov perfectly calibrated proxy residual SD phi > 1: excess variance beyond pcov predicts (biological scatter, plate effects, matrix interference) phi < 1: pcov overstates true residual variation (unusual) beta1 = gamma_1 precision exponent. beta1 = 1: theoretical delta-method prediction; sigma proportional pcov beta1 > 1: amplified weighting; high-pcov observations downweighted aggressively theory predicts beta1 < 1: compressed weighting; pcov gradient shallower theory predicts beta1 near 0: pcov carries almost information precision; weights near-uniform Use interpret_beta1() human-readable classification:
code: interpret_beta1(0.98, 0.85, 1.12) #> [1] "calibrated (pcov ~ residual SD)" interpret_beta1(2.01, 1.47, 2.57) #> [1] "amplified precision weighting" interpret_beta1(0.56, 0.30, 0.82) #> [1] "compressed precision weighting"

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: weight_diagnostics() provides quick summary much weights differentiate observations: Key metrics: n_eff: effective sample size; equals n weights uniform, decreases weights become heterogeneous eff_ratio: n_eff / n; 1.0 = uniform, lower = differentiation weight_ratio: max / min weight; large values indicate aggressive downweighting observations gini: Gini coefficient; 0 = uniform, approaching 1 = concentrated
code: # Example with known weights w <- c(rep(1.5, 50), rep(0.8, 30), rep(0.2, 20)) weight_diagnostics(w) #> $n_obs #> [1] 100 #>  #> $n_valid #> [1] 100 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 80.1 #>  #> $eff_ratio #> [1] 0.801 #>  #> $weight_ratio #> [1] 7.5 #>  #> $gini #> [1] 0.263

title: Introduction to curveRweights
text: default priors center beta1 1 SD = 0.5. antigens beta1 far 1 (like prn beta1 near 2), prior exerts meaningful shrinkage. Test sensitivity comparing results wider priors: beta1 shifts materially, data fully informative prior real work. wider prior gives weights better reflect data; narrower prior gives conservative (closer--uniform) weights.
code: # Default prior sw_default <- fit_saturated_weight(dat_prn, cell_col = "cell",                                     pcov_col = "pcov", plate_col = "plate")  # Wider prior sw_wide <- fit_saturated_weight(   dat_prn, cell_col = "cell", pcov_col = "pcov", plate_col = "plate",   prior_gamma1 = brms::set_prior("normal(1, 1.0)", class = "b", dpar = "sigma") )  cat("Default beta1:", sw_default$beta1, "\n") cat("Wide    beta1:", sw_wide$beta1, "\n")

title: Authors and Citation
text: Michael Scot Zens. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Zens M (2026). curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting Calibration Curve Uncertainty. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/immunoplex/curveRweights.
code: @Manual{,   title = {curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting from Calibration Curve Uncertainty},   author = {Michael Scot Zens},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/immunoplex/curveRweights}, }

title: curveRweights
text: Bayesian Precision Weighting Calibration Curve Uncertainty

title: curveRweights
text: Immunoassay measurements determined via calibration curve (typically nonlinear, sigmoidal function concentration assay response following logistic, log-logistic Gompertz form). observation posterior coefficient variation (pcov) quantifies precisely curve determines concentration. Observations near limits quantification (LLOQ, ULOQ) large pcov values curve flat ; midrange observations small pcov curve steep. Traditional approaches handle imprecision binary gate: observations LLOQ ULOQ included equal weight, others excluded. discards information (excluded observations still carry signal) treats included observations equally precise ().

title: curveRweights
text: curveRweights estimates continuous, power-law relationship pcov residual variance using joint Bayesian location-scale model: : phi (baseline scaling): captures variance inflation beyond pcov alone predicts — biological scatter, plate effects, matrix interference. phi = 1, pcov perfectly calibrated proxy residual SD. Values > 1 indicate excess variance (analogous positive tau-squared classical meta-analysis). beta1 (precision exponent): captures steeply pcov drives weight differentiation. beta1 = 1 theoretical delta-method prediction (sigma proportional pcov). beta1 > 1 means assay’s precision gradient steeper theory predicts (amplified weighting); beta1 < 1 means ’s shallower (compressed weighting). estimation uses saturated cell-means location model (one coefficient per experimental condition) scale parameters identified purely within-cell residual structure contaminated misspecified location effects.
code: sigma_i = phi * pcov_i^beta1   w_i     = 1 / sigma_i^2

title: curveRweights
text: classical random-effects meta-regression (DerSimonian & Laird, 1986), total per-observation variance decomposed tau2 + vi vi measurement error tau2 residual heterogeneity. curveRweights: phi^2 plays role tau2: global variance inflation beyond measurement error pcov_i^(2*beta1) plays role vi: per-observation measurement-error variance key difference phi scales entire sigma curve multiplicatively rather adding fixed floor, beta1 data-estimated rather fixed. using weights metafor::rma(), set method = "FE" (tau2 = 0) heterogeneity already captured phi.

title: curveRweights
code: # Install from GitHub (once published): # remotes::install_github("yourusername/curveRweights")  # Or install from local source during development: # In RStudio: Build > Install # Or from the command line: devtools::install()

title: curveRweights
text: curveRweights requires: R >= 4.1.0 brms >= 2.19.0 (Bayesian regression via Stan) dplyr >= 1.1.0 tibble Optional (downstream analysis): metafor (rma() meta-regression) survey (svyranktest() weighted rank tests)

title: curveRweights
code: library(curveRweights)  # Step 1: Define the saturated cell-means factor EXTERNALLY # This gives you full control over what "cells" means in your experiment. dat$cell <- interaction(dat$Arm, dat$timeperiod, drop = TRUE)  # Step 2: Fit per (antigen, source) group batch <- fit_saturated_weight_batch(   datg       = dat,   group_vars = c("antigen", "source"),   cell_col   = "cell",   pcov_col   = "pcov",   plate_col  = "plate",   iter       = 4000,   warmup     = 1000,   chains     = 4,   cores      = 4 )  # Step 3: Inspect scale estimates batch$scale_table  # Step 4: Use weights for any specific comparison dat_comparison <- batch$data |>   dplyr::filter(Arm %in% c("TT", "TdaP"), timeperiod == "post3rd")  # dat_comparison$w_saturated is ready for metafor::rma() or survey::svyranktest()

title: curveRweights
text: location model uses yi ~ 0 + cell (intercept; one coefficient per cell) rather structured model like yi ~ Arm * Timeperiod. : misspecification location model (e.g., assuming additivity ’s interaction) leave systematic patterns residuals contaminate scale estimates. saturated model absorbs systematic location variation construction, residuals cleanest possible input scale model. cell factor created externally, giving full control. can define match experimental design exactly.

what: Why the Cell Factor Is External
title: curveRweights
text: cell_col parameter takes pre-existing factor column rather Arm Timeperiod separately : Different experiments different designs (2 arms x 3 times, 4 arms x 1 time, dose-response x batch, etc.) function doesn’t need know cells represent — needs one coefficient per unique experimental condition can verify cell factor right levels passing long MCMC run

title: curveRweights
text: default priors : gamma_0 ~ Normal(0, 1): phi centered 1, 95% interval [0.14, 7.4] gamma_1 ~ Normal(1, 0.5): beta1 centered theory-predicted 1, 95% interval [0, 2] groups beta1 far 1 (e.g., > 2), prior exerts meaningful shrinkage. prior_gamma1 parameter allows pass custom prior sensitivity analysis:
code: # Wider prior for groups with large beta1 sw <- fit_saturated_weight(   df = dat,   cell_col = "cell",   prior_gamma1 = brms::prior(normal(1, 1.0), class = "b", dpar = "sigma") )

title: curveRweights
text: Burkner P-C (2017). brms: R Package Bayesian Multilevel Models Using Stan. Journal Statistical Software, 80(1), 1-28. DOI: 10.18637/jss.v080.i01 Burkner P-C (2018). what: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
title: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
text: scale estimates previous fit (via fit_saturated_weight() fit_saturated_weight_batch()) want apply new subsetted data frame without re-fitting brms model.

title: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
code: apply_saturated_weights(   df,   scale_table,   group_vars = c("antigen", "source"),   concentration_col = "predicted_concentration",   se_col = "se_concentration",   pcov_col = "pcov" )

title: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
text: df Data frame receive weights. scale_table scale_table fit_saturated_weight_batch(), one-row tibble/data.frame least phi beta1 (gamma_0 gamma_1). group_vars Character vector: column names match df scale_table group-specific scale parameters. NULL = apply single global (phi, beta1) rows. concentration_col Character: predicted concentration column name. se_col Character: SE concentration column name. pcov_col Character: posterior CV column name.

title: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
text: input data frame added columns: yi, cv_i, log_cv, cv_source, sigma_i, w_saturated, w_saturated_norm.

title: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
text: Stage 2 function recommended two-stage workflow: fit full dataset (arms, timepoints), apply weights comparison subset.

title: Apply Previously Estimated Scale Parameters to New Data — apply_saturated_weights
code: # \donttest{ data(example_assay)  # Fit on one group first dat_prn <- example_assay[example_assay$antigen == "prn" &                          example_assay$feature == "IgG1", ] dat_prn$cell <- interaction(dat_prn$group_a, dat_prn$group_b, drop = TRUE) sw <- fit_saturated_weight(dat_prn, cell_col = "cell", pcov_col = "pcov",                            plate_col = "plate",                            iter = 1000, warmup = 500, chains = 2, cores = 2) #> fit_saturated_weight: 506 of 512 observations usable (6 removed); 8 cell levels #>   cv: OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.487; beta1 identifiable from 506 observations #>   location: yi ~ 0 + cell + (1 | plate) #>   scale:    sigma ~ log_cv #>   fitting brms model (1000 iter, 2 chains)... #> Warning: Bulk Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior means and medians may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#bulk-ess #>   sigma fixef rows: sigma_Intercept, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_4, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_4, sigma_log_cv #>   sigma fixef cols: Estimate, Est.Error, Q2.5, Q97.5 #>   phi = 2.885  [2.03, 4.07] #>   beta1 = 1.024  [0.829, 1.2] #>   interpretation: moderate precision weighting #>   n_eff = 415.1 of 506 (ratio = 0.82) #>   weight_ratio = 1027  gini = 0.259  # Build a scale_table manually (or use batch$scale_table) st <- data.frame(antigen = "prn", phi = sw$phi, beta1 = sw$beta1)  # Apply to new data without re-fitting dat_new <- example_assay[example_assay$antigen == "prn" &                          example_assay$feature == "IgG1" &                          example_assay$group_b == "timepoint_3", ] dat_weighted <- apply_saturated_weights(   df          = dat_new,   scale_table = st,   group_vars  = "antigen",   pcov_col    = "pcov" ) summary(dat_weighted$w_saturated_norm) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's  #>  0.0302  0.8877  1.0943  1.0000  1.2029  1.3718       2  # }

what: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
title: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
text: Given estimated log-scale intercept (gamma_0) slope (gamma_1), computes per-observation residual SD (sigma_i), raw precision weight (w_saturated), mean-normalised weight (w_saturated_norm).

title: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
code: compute_saturated_weights(df, gamma_0, gamma_1 = 0)

title: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
text: df Data frame log_cv column (prepare_cv()). gamma_0 Numeric: estimated intercept log-sigma model (log(phi)). gamma_1 Numeric: estimated slope log-sigma model (beta1). Set 0 intercept-(uniform weights).  Default 0.

title: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
text: input data frame additional columns: sigma_i Estimated residual SD observation. w_saturated Raw precision weight: 1 / sigma_i^2. w_saturated_norm Mean-normalised weight: w_saturated / mean(w_saturated). shape identical w_saturated; normalisation mean = 1 applied cross-group comparability.

title: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
text: transformation : $$\sigma_i = \exp(\gamma_0 + \gamma_1 \cdot \log(\mathrm{cv}_i))$$ $$w_i = 1 / \sigma_i^2$$

title: Compute Precision Weights from Estimated Scale Parameters — compute_saturated_weights
code: data(example_assay) dat_sub <- example_assay[example_assay$antigen == "prn" &                          example_assay$feature == "IgG1", ] d <- prepare_cv(dat_sub, pcov_col = "pcov")  # Apply hypothetical scale estimates d <- compute_saturated_weights(d, gamma_0 = 0.5, gamma_1 = 1.2) summary(d$w_saturated_norm) #>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max.      NA's  #> 0.0005172 0.5825621 1.1687081 1.0000000 1.4015256 1.7504343         6  weight_diagnostics(d$w_saturated) #> $n_obs #> [1] 512 #>  #> $n_valid #> [1] 506 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 402.3 #>  #> $eff_ratio #> [1] 0.795 #>  #> $weight_ratio #> [1] 3384 #>  #> $gini #> [1] 0.284 #>

what: curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting from Calibration Curve Uncertainty — curveRweights-package
title: curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting from Calibration Curve Uncertainty — curveRweights-package
text: #' Immunoassay observations measured via calibration curve (typically nonlinear, sigmoidal function concentration assay response following logistic, log-logistic Gompertz form). observation posterior coefficient variation (pcov) quantifies precisely curve determines concentration. curveRweights estimates power-law relationship pcov residual variance using joint Bayesian location-scale model: $$\sigma_i = \phi \cdot \mathrm{pcov}_i^{\beta_1}$$ $$w_i = 1 / \sigma_i^2$$ : phi Baseline scaling factor (exp log-scale intercept). Values > 1 indicate excess variance beyond pcov predicts — biological scatter, plate effects, matrix interference. beta1 Precision exponent.  beta1 = 1 means pcov direct proxy residual SD (delta-method prediction).  beta1 > 1 means amplified weighting; beta1 < 1 means compressed weighting.

title: curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting from Calibration Curve Uncertainty — curveRweights-package
text: classical random-effects meta-regression (DerSimonian & Laird, 1986), total per-observation variance decomposed \(\tau^2 + v_i\) \(v_i\) measurement error \(\tau^2\) residual heterogeneity.  curveRweights model, \(\sigma_i^2\) plays role \((\tau^2 + v_i)\) simultaneously: \(\phi^2\) captures \(\tau^2\) captured globally, \(\mathrm{pcov}_i^{2\beta_1}\) captures \(v_i\) captured locally.  Consequently, using weights rma, set method = "FE" (tau2 fixed 0) avoid double-counting heterogeneity.

what: Independence of scale and location estimates
title: curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting from Calibration Curve Uncertainty — curveRweights-package
text: Fisher information scale parameters depends distribution log(pcov), experimental condition labels location-model coefficients.  scale estimates therefore independent treatment effect conditional pcov.  independence holds pcov calibration-curve property (well 4PL determined concentration), biological outcome treatment.  verified empirically checking pcov distributions balanced across experimental conditions.

title: curveRweights: Bayesian Precision Weighting from Calibration Curve Uncertainty — curveRweights-package
code: library(curveRweights)  # 1. Define the saturated cell-means factor externally dat$cell <- interaction(dat$Arm, dat$timeperiod, drop = TRUE)  # 2. Fit per (antigen, source) group batch <- fit_saturated_weight_batch(   datg       = dat,   group_vars = c("antigen", "source"),   cell_col   = "cell",   pcov_col   = "pcov",   plate_col  = "plate" )  # 3. Use weights for any comparison dat_sub <- batch$data |>   dplyr::filter(Arm %in% c("TT", "TdaP"), timeperiod == "post3rd") # dat_sub$w_saturated is ready for rma() or svyranktest()

what: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
title: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
text: scale submodel log(sigma) ~ log_cv requires meaningful spread log_cv across observations.  observations nearly identical pcov (e.g., well-determined midrange), beta1 identifiable model falls back intercept-sigma (uniform weights).

title: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
code: diagnose_cv(df)

title: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
text: df Data frame cv_i log_cv columns, typically prepare_cv().

title: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
text: Named list: n_finite Number observations finite cv_i > 0. cv_min, cv_median, cv_max Summary statistics cv_i. log_cv_sd Standard deviation log_cv. log_cv_range Range (max - min) log_cv. use_cv_slope Logical: TRUE sd(log_cv) >= 0.05. message Human-readable summary diagnosis.

title: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
text: threshold sd(log_cv) >= 0.05.  , enough contrast measurement precision across concentration range distinguish differential weighting uniform weighting.

title: Diagnose Precision Index Variation for Scale Estimation — diagnose_cv
code:
data(example_assay) dat_sub <- example_assay[example_assay$antigen == "prn" &                          example_assay$feature == "IgG1", ] d <- prepare_cv(dat_sub, pcov_col = "pcov") cv_diag <- diagnose_cv(d) cat(cv_diag$message, "
") #> OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.487; beta1 identifiable from 506 observations  cat("sd(log_cv) =", cv_diag$log_cv_sd, "
") #> sd(log_cv) = 0.4872  cat("use slope?", cv_diag$use_cv_slope, "
") #> use slope? TRUE

what: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay
title: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay
text: Anonymised observation-level data Luminex multiplex immunoassay measuring antibody responses across two vaccine groups, four timepoints, eleven antigens, ten immunological features.  observation includes predicted concentration semi-supervised, best fitting calibration curve, standard error, posterior coefficient variation (pcov) quantifies calibration curve uncertainty. calculations computed -SPI using bayesian regression approach (https://immunoplex.org/-spi-docs) .

title: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay
code: example_assay

title: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay
text: data frame 48,224 rows 11 columns: plate Character. Plate identifier (15 plates: plate_1 plate_15). nominal_sample_dilution Character. Anonymised dilution factor (8 levels: dil_1 dil_8). patientid Character. Anonymised subject identifier (150 subjects: S001 S150). group_a Character. Vaccine group (2 levels: vaccine_a, vaccine_b). treatment arm used independent variable arm-effect comparisons. group_b Character. Timepoint (4 levels: timepoint_1 timepoint_4, chronological order). antigen Character. Target antigen (11 levels: act, dt, fha, fim, ipv1, ipv2, ipv3, pentamer, prn, pt, tt). feature Character. Immunological readout (10 levels: ADCD, ADCP, ADNP, FcgR2a, FcgR3b, IgG1, IgG2, IgG3, IgG4, Total_IgG). mfi Numeric. Median fluorescence intensity (raw assay readout). predicted_concentration Numeric. Concentration predicted 4PL calibration curve (arbitrary units). se_concentration Numeric. Standard error predicted concentration calibration curve posterior. pcov Numeric. Posterior coefficient variation calibration curve: se_concentration / predicted_concentration midrange samples, correctly capturing non-Gaussian posterior near LLOQ ULOQ.  precision index used curveRweights estimate observation-level weights.

title: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay
text: Anonymised maternal pertussis vaccine immunogenicity study.

title: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay
text: data anonymised replacing patient IDs random codes (S001 S150, shuffled), sample dilutions generic labels (dil_1 dil_8), timepoint names sequential labels (timepoint_1 timepoint_4). measurement values (mfi, predicted_concentration, se_concentration, pcov) unchanged. Typical usage selects one (antigen, feature) combination analysis. example, IgG1 responses pertussis antigens (pt, fha, prn) natural starting point:
code: dat_sub <- example_assay |>   dplyr::filter(antigen == "prn", feature == "IgG1")

title: Example Luminex Multiplex Immunoassay Data — example_assay

what: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
title: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
text: Fits brms Gaussian location-scale model location uses saturated cell means (one coefficient per level cell_col) absorb systematic variation response, scale estimates power-law relationship calibration curve's posterior CV (pcov) residual variance, shared across cells.

title: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
code: fit_saturated_weight(   df,   cell_col = "cell",   concentration_col = "predicted_concentration",   se_col = "se_concentration",   pcov_col = "pcov",   plate_col = "plate",   prior_gamma0 = brms::set_prior("normal(0, 1)", class = "Intercept", dpar = "sigma"),   prior_gamma1 = brms::set_prior("normal(1, 0.5)", class = "b", dpar = "sigma"),   prior_location = brms::set_prior("normal(0, 2)", class = "b"),   prior_plate_sd = brms::set_prior("normal(0, 0.5)", class = "sd"),   iter = 4000,   warmup = 1000,   chains = 4,   cores = 4,   adapt_delta = 0.95,   seed = 42 )

title: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
text: df Data frame observation-level data. cell_col Character: name saturated cell-means factor column. Create externally, e.g.: df$cell <- interaction(df$Arm, df$Timeperiod, drop = TRUE). concentration_col Character: predicted concentration column name. se_col Character: SE concentration column name. pcov_col Character: posterior CV column name. NULL = compute se/conc. plate_col Character: plate column name random intercept. NULL = plate random effect. prior_gamma0 brms::prior object gamma_0 (log phi). prior_gamma1 brms::prior object gamma_1 (beta1). prior_location brms::prior object location (cell mean) coefficients. prior_plate_sd brms::prior object plate RE standard deviation. iter Integer: MCMC iterations per chain. warmup Integer: warmup iterations per chain. chains Integer: number MCMC chains. cores Integer: parallel cores chain computation. adapt_delta Numeric: target HMC acceptance probability (0-1). seed Integer: random seed reproducibility.

title: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
text: Named list elements: fit (brmsfit object), phi (baseline scaling factor), beta1 (precision exponent), gamma_0 gamma_1 (log-scale estimates), gamma_0_CI gamma_1_CI (95 percent credible intervals), phi_CI (back-transformed CI phi), interpretation (precision regime label), effective_se_power (2 * beta1), data (input data weights added: yi, cv_i, log_cv, sigma_i, w_saturated, w_saturated_norm), diagnostics (convergence weight diagnostics), cv_diagnostics (output diagnose_cv), formula (location scale formula strings), priors_used (priors passed brms).

title: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
text: location model uses yi ~ 0 + cell [+ (1|plate)] (saturated cell means).  scale model uses log(sigma) = gamma_0 + gamma_1 * log(cv). Default prior gamma_0 Normal(0, 1), centering phi = exp(gamma_0) 1. Default prior gamma_1 Normal(1, 0.5), centering beta1 delta-method prediction 1, 95 percent interval 0 2.

title: Fit a Bayesian Location-Scale Model with Saturated Location and Shared Scale — fit_saturated_weight
code: # \donttest{ data(example_assay)  # Select one antigen/feature group and create the cell factor dat_prn <- example_assay[example_assay$antigen == "prn" &                          example_assay$feature == "IgG1", ] dat_prn$cell <- interaction(dat_prn$group_a, dat_prn$group_b, drop = TRUE)  # Fit (reduced iterations for speed; use 4000/1000 for real analysis) sw <- fit_saturated_weight(   df        = dat_prn,   cell_col  = "cell",   pcov_col  = "pcov",   plate_col = "plate",   iter = 1000, warmup = 500, chains = 2, cores = 2 ) #> fit_saturated_weight: 506 of 512 observations usable (6 removed); 8 cell levels #>   cv: OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.487; beta1 identifiable from 506 observations #>   location: yi ~ 0 + cell + (1 | plate) #>   scale:    sigma ~ log_cv #>   fitting brms model (1000 iter, 2 chains)... #> Warning: Bulk Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior means and medians may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#bulk-ess #>   sigma fixef rows: sigma_Intercept, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_4, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_4, sigma_log_cv #>   sigma fixef cols: Estimate, Est.Error, Q2.5, Q97.5 #>   phi = 2.885  [2.03, 4.07] #>   beta1 = 1.024  [0.829, 1.2] #>   interpretation: moderate precision weighting #>   n_eff = 415.1 of 506 (ratio = 0.82) #>   weight_ratio = 1027  gini = 0.259  cat("phi =", sw$phi, " beta1 =", sw$beta1, "\n") #> phi = 2.884783  beta1 = 1.023849  cat(sw$interpretation, "\n") #> moderate precision weighting   # Weights are on sw$data summary(sw$data$w_saturated_norm) #>     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.     NA's  #> 0.001607 0.645223 1.168663 1.000000 1.364593 1.649590        6  weight_diagnostics(sw$data$w_saturated) #> $n_obs #> [1] 512 #>  #> $n_valid #> [1] 506 #>  #> $n_eff #> [1] 415.1 #>  #> $eff_ratio #> [1] 0.82 #>  #> $weight_ratio #> [1] 1027 #>  #> $gini #> [1] 0.259 #>  # }

what: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
title: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
text: Loops groups defined group_vars (e.g., antigen x source) fits one fit_saturated_weight() model per group.  Returns combined data frame weights summary table scale estimates.

title: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
code: fit_saturated_weight_batch(   datg,   group_vars = c("antigen", "source"),   cell_col = "cell",   ... )

title: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
text: datg Data frame containing groups. group_vars Character vector: column names defining groups. unique combination gets model. cell_col Character: name saturated cell-means factor (created externally). ... Additional arguments passed fit_saturated_weight(), pcov_col, plate_col, iter, warmup, chains, cores.

title: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
text: Named list: data Full data frame w_saturated w_saturated_norm columns added (groups combined). scale_table tibble one row per group containing: group key columns, phi, beta1, credible intervals, interpretation, n_fit, n_eff, weight_ratio. fits Named list brmsfit objects indexed group label. diagnostics Named list per-group diagnostic lists.

title: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
text: group gets (phi, beta1) pcov--variance relationship may differ across antigens (different 4PL curve shapes) standard curve sources (different concentration ranges).

title: Fit Saturated Weight Models Across Multiple Groups — fit_saturated_weight_batch
code: # \donttest{ data(example_assay)  # Select IgG1 for pertussis antigens dat_igg1 <- example_assay[example_assay$feature == "IgG1" &                           example_assay$antigen %in% c("pt", "fha", "prn"), ] dat_igg1$cell <- interaction(dat_igg1$group_a, dat_igg1$group_b, drop = TRUE)  # Fit across antigens (reduced iterations for speed) batch <- fit_saturated_weight_batch(   datg       = dat_igg1,   group_vars = c("antigen"),   cell_col   = "cell",   pcov_col   = "pcov",   plate_col  = "plate",   iter = 1000, warmup = 500, chains = 2, cores = 2 ) #> fit_saturated_weight_batch: 3 groups #>  #> [1/3] antigen=prn #> fit_saturated_weight: 506 of 512 observations usable (6 removed); 8 cell levels #>   cv: OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.487; beta1 identifiable from 506 observations #>   location: yi ~ 0 + cell + (1 | plate) #>   scale:    sigma ~ log_cv #>   fitting brms model (1000 iter, 2 chains)... #> Warning: Bulk Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior means and medians may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#bulk-ess #>   sigma fixef rows: sigma_Intercept, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_4, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_4, sigma_log_cv #>   sigma fixef cols: Estimate, Est.Error, Q2.5, Q97.5 #>   phi = 2.885  [2.03, 4.07] #>   beta1 = 1.024  [0.829, 1.2] #>   interpretation: moderate precision weighting #>   n_eff = 415.1 of 506 (ratio = 0.82) #>   weight_ratio = 1027  gini = 0.259 #>  #> [2/3] antigen=pt #> fit_saturated_weight: 511 of 512 observations usable (1 removed); 8 cell levels #>   cv: OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.605; beta1 identifiable from 511 observations #>   location: yi ~ 0 + cell + (1 | plate) #>   scale:    sigma ~ log_cv #>   fitting brms model (1000 iter, 2 chains)... #>   sigma fixef rows: sigma_Intercept, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_4, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_4, sigma_log_cv #>   sigma fixef cols: Estimate, Est.Error, Q2.5, Q97.5 #>   phi = 2.234  [1.5, 3.55] #>   beta1 = 0.7743  [0.634, 0.935] #>   interpretation: compressed precision weighting #>   n_eff = 411.6 of 511 (ratio = 0.805) #>   weight_ratio = 859.6  gini = 0.277 #>  #> [3/3] antigen=fha #> fit_saturated_weight: 511 of 512 observations usable (1 removed); 8 cell levels #>   cv: OK: sd(log_cv) = 0.546; beta1 identifiable from 511 observations #>   location: yi ~ 0 + cell + (1 | plate) #>   scale:    sigma ~ log_cv #>   fitting brms model (1000 iter, 2 chains)... #> Warning: Bulk Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior means and medians may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#bulk-ess #> Warning: Tail Effective Samples Size (ESS) is too low, indicating posterior variances and tail quantiles may be unreliable. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#tail-ess #>   sigma fixef rows: sigma_Intercept, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_1, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_2, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_3, cellvaccine_a.timepoint_4, cellvaccine_b.timepoint_4, sigma_log_cv #>   sigma fixef cols: Estimate, Est.Error, Q2.5, Q97.5 #>   phi = 3.228  [2.26, 4.61] #>   beta1 = 1.034  [0.904, 1.16] #>   interpretation: calibrated (pcov ~ residual SD) #>   n_eff = 288.3 of 511 (ratio = 0.564) #>   weight_ratio = 24060  gini = 0.469  # Scale summary: one row per antigen batch$scale_table #>   antigen      phi     beta1   phi_lo   phi_hi  beta1_lo  beta1_hi #> 1     prn 2.884783 1.0238494 2.030100 4.074802 0.8291515 1.2042617 #> 2      pt 2.234074 0.7743324 1.502670 3.549606 0.6339866 0.9351635 #> 3     fha 3.227635 1.0338441 2.264336 4.611407 0.9043599 1.1608369 #>                    interpretation n_fit n_eff weight_ratio #> 1    moderate precision weighting   506 415.1       1027.0 #> 2  compressed precision weighting   511 411.6        859.6 #> 3 calibrated (pcov ~ residual SD)   511 288.3      24060.0  # Weighted data for one comparison dat_comparison <- batch$data[batch$data$group_a %in% c("vaccine_a", "vaccine_b") &                              batch$data$group_b == "timepoint_3", ] nrow(dat_comparison) #> [1] 450 # }

what: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
title: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
text: Classifies pcov--variance power-law exponent interpretable regimes based estimated beta1 credible interval.

title: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
code: interpret_beta1(beta1, beta1_lo = NA_real_, beta1_hi = NA_real_)

title: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
text: beta1 Numeric: estimated beta1 (gamma_1 log-scale model). beta1_lo Numeric: lower bound 95\ Default NA_real_. beta1_hi Numeric: upper bound 95\ Default NA_real_.

title: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
text: Character string describing precision weighting regime: "identified (uniform precision)" beta1 NA "near-uniform weights" beta1 < 0.2 "compressed precision weighting" 0.2 <= beta1 < 0.8 "calibrated (pcov ~ residual SD)" CI contained within 0.8 1.2 "amplified precision weighting" beta1 > 1.2 "moderate precision weighting" cases

title: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
text: theoretical prediction delta method applied 4PL calibration curve beta1 = 1 (pcov direct proxy residual SD log10-concentration scale).  Departures 1 indicate assay's measurement precision maps residual variance different power theory predicts.

title: Interpret the Estimated Beta1 Value — interpret_beta1
code: interpret_beta1(0.98, 0.85, 1.12) #> [1] "calibrated (pcov ~ residual SD)" interpret_beta1(2.01, 1.47, 2.57) #> [1] "amplified precision weighting" interpret_beta1(0.56, 0.30, 0.82) #> [1] "compressed precision weighting"

what: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv
title: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv
text: Computes precision index (cv_i) log transform (log_cv) used predictor scale submodel.  Uses stored posterior CV (pcov) calibration curve available; falls back se_concentration / predicted_concentration otherwise.

title: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv
code: prepare_cv(   df,   concentration_col = "predicted_concentration",   se_col = "se_concentration",   pcov_col = "pcov" )

title: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv
text: df Data frame observation-level data. concentration_col Character: name predicted concentration column. se_col Character: name SE concentration column. pcov_col Character: name posterior CV column.  Set NULL force computation se_col / concentration_col.

title: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv
text: input data frame additional columns: yi log10(predicted_concentration).  NA concentration non-finite non-positive. cv_i Precision index: pcov available finite, else se/conc.  NA neither source usable. log_cv log(cv_i).  NA cv_i non-finite non-positive. cv_source Character indicating source used row: "pcov" "se_over_conc".

title: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv
text: pcov preferred correctly captures non-Gaussian posterior near LLOQ ULOQ standard calibration curve flat delta-method se/conc approximation breaks .

title: Prepare the Precision Index from Calibration Curve Output — prepare_cv

what: Compute Summary Diagnostics for Precision Weights — weight_diagnostics
title: Compute Summary Diagnostics for Precision Weights — weight_diagnostics
text: Summarises distribution effective information content set precision weights.

title: Compute Summary Diagnostics for Precision Weights — weight_diagnostics
code: weight_diagnostics(w)

title: Compute Summary Diagnostics for Precision Weights — weight_diagnostics
text: w Numeric vector weights (e.g., w_saturated w_saturated_norm).

title: Compute Summary Diagnostics for Precision Weights — weight_diagnostics
text: Named list: n_obs Total observations (including NA). n_valid Observations finite, positive weights. n_eff Effective sample size: \([\sum w_i]^2 / \sum w_i^2\). Equals n_valid weights equal; decreases weights become heterogeneous. eff_ratio n_eff / n_valid.  Ranges 0 1; 1 = uniform. weight_ratio max(w) / min(w) among valid weights. gini Gini coefficient weights.  0 = perfectly uniform, approaching 1 = highly concentrated.

title: Compute Summary Diagnostics for Precision Weights — weight_diagnostics
